meta.method: Two multi-agent review workflows (9 + 14 dimensions, adversarial verification, completeness critics) merged with a full independent file-by-file read; severities adjudicated by hand; several agent claims tempered or refuted.

fixes_applied.tests: tests/test_fixes_rt01_08.py (39 new cases); full suite 66 passed
fixes_applied.verification: adversarial review workflow (8 per-finding verifiers + regression hunter); RT-06 re-hardened and re-verified after the first pass flagged it

fix_batches.tests: tests/test_fixes_rt09_15.py (18 cases); full suite 84 passed; ruff clean
fix_batches.verification: adversarial workflow (6 finders + regression hunter); RT-12 (naive-datetime crash) and RT-15 (empty-list->inherit-all) found and fixed, then re-verified
fix_batches.residual: RT-23 (writable HOME for SDK state) surfaced as the next blocker to a live container run

fix_batches.tests: tests/test_fixes_rt23.py (21 cases) + tests/container/smoke_rt23.sh (real docker compose run); full suite 105 passed; ruff clean
fix_batches.verification: adversarial workflow (renderer-security + entrypoint/container + regression hunter); F1 (overlapping allow-list -> nft reject -> boot DoS) and F2 (string-vs-parsed IMDS scrub bypassable by non-canonical IPv6) found and fixed, then re-verified in-container; engagement-path single-sourcing added after the entrypoint reviewer flagged netpolicy/CLI could read two different engagements
fix_batches.residual: RT-23 fully fixed and live-container-verified; the harness can now complete read-only-rootfs startup. Remaining open findings are the lower-severity hardening sweep (RT-11, RT-16..RT-22, RT-24..RT-31).

fix_batches.batch: M0+M1 (runtime runnable + contained-engagement trust; closes RT-21, CLI-run slice of RT-26)
fix_batches.tests: tests/test_m0_doctor.py (18), test_m1_report.py (6), test_m1_cage.py (4), test_m1_engagement.py (1), test_m1_cli_failclosed.py (2); full suite 135 passed; ruff clean; in-container `docker compose run redteam doctor [--probe]` proves the SDK spawns the claude CLI transport under read-only rootfs
fix_batches.verification: adversarial workflow (M0 runtime/doctor + M1 report/cage/RT-26 + regression hunter); fixed: probe orphaning the claude subprocess on timeout (connect/disconnect + shielded teardown), cli_missing-vs-sdk_missing mislabel, find_cli diverging from the SDK's fallback paths, doctor --probe phoning home with no egress box (CLI telemetry/autoupdater disabled at image level), a tautological cage assertion, brittle version parsing, and a corrupt-SARIF-base edge
fix_batches.scope: M0: Dockerfile ships Node + pinned @anthropic-ai/claude-code (the SDK transport); new `redteam doctor [--probe]` readiness command + redteam/preflight.py; entrypoint gates the egress netpolicy + engagement-append to engagement-bearing subcommands only. M1: report SARIF write is atomic + lock-serialized (RT-21); CLI run setup fails closed (RT-26 CLI slice); cage-validation and end-to-end seal/verify/SARIF pinned as contract tests; engagements/whitebox-first.example.yaml for the first contained run.
fix_batches.live_run: FIRST REAL AUTONOMOUS ENGAGEMENT COMPLETED (2026-06-26, host headless `claude` 2.1.191). The agent autonomously reviewed a planted-vulnerable backend, the cage denied ToolSearch/Read and allowed the whitelisted whitebox/report tools LIVE, and it recorded 6 correct findings (SQLi, broken access control, unauth PII export, hardcoded Stripe key + DB password, Flask debug RCE) via report__write_finding -> 6 SARIF results + 6 finding.recorded ledger entries; the 42-entry hash-chained ledger sealed and `redteam-verify` passed. The first run also surfaced a CRITICAL runtime-only bug all 138 unit tests missed: the SDK invokes a tool handler with a single args dict and expects {'content':[...]} back, but our handlers took unpacked params and returned raw dicts -> every tool crashed. Fixed with a dual-convention adapter in redteam/tools/_sdk_shim.py (single-dict in -> unpack -> MCP content out; kwargs path preserved for tests), pinned by tests/test_m1_tool_sdk_contract.py, and re-verified by a successful re-run.
fix_batches.residual: The live run was headless on the host's authenticated CLI; the *container* live run additionally needs the secret->env wiring + asset-mount reconciliation + backend egress host (the credentialed container hand-off). The agent still reports findings well but finding capture is prompt-driven, not structurally enforced (motivates the M3 verify/report pipeline). RT-17 (ledger/budget lock), RT-20 (PostToolUse fired 11x for 19 allowed calls — budget/telemetry semantics), and the seal-swallow slice of RT-26 remain open.

fix_batches.batch: M3 (the `redteam triage` findings-quality pipeline — the workflow-shaped trust backend)
fix_batches.tests: 12 new test files tests/test_m3_*.py (123 cases, incl. test_m3_hardening.py for the review defects); full suite 266 passed; ruff clean. Real deterministic run over audit/whitebox-first/ENG-WHITEBOX-FIRST.jsonl emits a schema-valid triaged SARIF + markdown + triage.json (kept=5, 1 duplicate collapsed).
fix_batches.verification: five-reviewer adversarial workflow (data-layer, prefilter/dedup/enrich, verify/chain/llm, emit/CLI, regression+spec-acceptance) then a focused re-verifier over the fixes. Read-only-over-ledger confirmed by hashing the real + synthetic ledgers before/after a run; CVSS 3.1 base score cross-checked against a fully independent reimplementation over all 2592 metric combinations (0 mismatches).
fix_batches.scope: New redteam/pipeline/ package (models, load, cwe, cvss, stages, emit, llm) + `redteam triage <ledger>` CLI. Deterministic stages (prefilter -> dedup -> enrich -> emit) are credential-free and total (never raise on a bad finding — drop + record); opt-in adversarial verify (confidence-gated) and exploit-chain synthesis are gated on preflight.detect_backend and driven over the SDK one-shot query(). report.py refactor: exported SARIF_LEVEL + factored _atomic_write_text (reused by emit for atomic markdown/JSON).
fix_batches.hardening: 14 defects the adversarial review found, all fixed + regression-tested: extract_json O(N^2) hang + RecursionError escape on adversarial model output -> bounded raw_decode extractor; parsed_location ValueError on a >4300-digit line (Py3.14 int cap) -> bounded regex; SARIF startLine:0 / reversed-range emitted a schema-invalid document -> _physical clamps to the SARIF region invariants; markdown location-cell injection -> _md_cell escaping; verdict laundering via a conflicting bottom-up VERDICT line -> conflicting verdicts degrade to UNVERIFIED (never elevated to TP nor dropped as FP); missing-confidence TRUE_POSITIVE dropped -> kept UNVERIFIED (no false negative); enrich trusting a ledger-supplied cvss_vector -> load whitelists only the agent's report fields; plus is_file() ENAMETOOLONG, non-UTF8 ledger byte, engagement-id precedence, cwe 'mon-key' mishit, and dead dedup code.
fix_batches.live_run: LIVE verify+chain confirmed against the host authenticated `claude` (2026-07-02): triage over the 6-finding reference ledger verified all 5 canonical findings TRUE_POSITIVE (precision 1.0) and synthesised 3 validated critical exploit chains (unauth SQLi->full DB exfiltration, SQLi->Werkzeug-debugger RCE, debug-traceback disclosure->stolen prod DB creds) in ~83s. The live check exposed a bug NO mock could: the one-shot SDK query() inherits the host CLI's built-in tools, so a 'walk the callers' verify prompt made the model attempt tool_use and exhaust max_turns=1 -> every verify errored -> all findings UNVERIFIED/degraded. Fixed by granting the reasoning turn NO tools (allowed_tools=[] in llm._query_options — which also enforces the pipeline's source-containment) and rewording the verify/chain prompts to reason only from the provided excerpt; a clean re-run confirmed precision 1.0.
fix_batches.residual: v-next (documented non-goals, marked in code/spec): semantic/LLM dedup, CMDB/environmental CVSS + offensive-priority, Jira/Atlassian upsert of triaged findings, multi-backend routing. Spec AC1 literally said 'assert 6 kept' over a copied live ledger; per session direction the committed fixture is synthetic (a duplicate pair + a no-evidence finding, no real secret strings) so the deterministic run is 4 kept / 2 dropped — same intent (deterministic enrich + dedup + prefilter, 3 artifacts, credential-free), broader coverage. RESOLVED (user decision): the --verify/--chain gate now uses preflight.model_stage_ready — an env backend OR a present `claude` CLI (login/session auth) satisfies it, mirroring the `run` path; it refuses only when there is neither. Confirmed live: `redteam triage --verify --chain` over the reference ledger on the login-only host verifies precision 1.0 + 3 chains, no degradation, all three artifacts emitted.

fix_batches.tests: tests/test_kms_sealer_switch.py (10 cases: build_sealer factory, LedgerWriter sealer-injection + precedence, KMS<->file round trip through verify.py, orchestrator wiring); full suite 280 passed; ruff clean.
fix_batches.verification: three-agent adversarial pass (seal/verify correctness + fail-closed, regression hunter, hard-constraint/CLAUDE.md compliance). All clean; only a doc-sync flag (CLAUDE.md still listed kms_seal.py as stubbed) which this batch fixes. Regression hunter confirmed the new `sealer` param sits between two keyword-with-default args and no caller passes Orchestrator/LedgerWriter args positionally past that point, and re-ran the full suite (280 passed).
fix_batches.scope: redteam/ledger/kms_seal.py: added `Sealer.write_seal` to the protocol, `KmsHmacSealer.write_seal()` (owns the KMS seal-file format, delegated to by the retained `write_kms_seal` free fn), and `build_sealer(env)` (KMS when REDTEAM_KMS_KEY_ID set, region from REDTEAM_KMS_REGION|AWS_REGION|AWS_DEFAULT_REGION, else None). redteam/ledger/chain.py: LedgerWriter gained a `sealer` param authoritative over `hmac_key`; seal() delegates to it or falls back to the file-key path, which now records `method: "file"` (was implicit); raises when neither present. redteam/orchestrator.py: `sealer` param threaded into LedgerWriter. redteam/cli.py: `run` injects `build_sealer(os.environ)` at the CLI edge (mirrors `load_hmac_key()`).
fix_batches.fail_closed: seal() still raises RuntimeError only when BOTH sealer and hmac_key are absent (pinned). Orchestrator.seal() swallows only that RuntimeError; a genuine kms:GenerateMac failure (botocore errors extend Exception, not RuntimeError) propagates rather than silently leaving an unsealed production ledger. boto3 import stays inside KmsHmacSealer._client (import-safe; no mocked test makes a live AWS call). Empty region surfaces as a boto3 error at sign time, never a silent mis-seal.
fix_batches.residual: A live KMS seal is unexercisable here (host has no AWS creds); boto3-mocked tests cover sign/verify/write_seal/round-trip. The runtime docker-compose already sets REDTEAM_KMS_REGION=${AWS_REGION:-us-east-1}. Switching the default sealer to KMS in-container needs the workload IAM role (kms:GenerateMac on the key ARN) at deploy time.

fix_batches.tests: tests/test_recon_gh.py (22 cases: argv-building + JSON parse for all 3 tools; degrade-never-crash for missing-binary/non-zero-exit/timeout/non-JSON; argument-injection guard; scope-broadening query-qualifier rejection; owner allowlist enforcement; non-integer limit; regex trailing-newline; github_orgs schema validation). Full suite 302 passed; ruff clean.
fix_batches.verification: two-agent adversarial pass (security/injection+containment, regression/correctness). No command/flag injection found (list argv, leading-'-' rejected, guards run before gh). The security reviewer surfaced 3 real gaps in the guarantees the docstring CLAIMED, all fixed in this batch: (F1) org-scoping was not engagement-bound and a query could carry org:/user:/repo: qualifiers to escape --owner; (F2) int(limit) sat outside try/except -> non-int raised, contradicting 'total, never raise'; (F3) regex '$' accepted a trailing newline. Regression reviewer confirmed no existing test pins the recon pack's tool set, orchestrator grants mcp__recon (whole server), all 3 names spelled identically in _TARGETLESS_TOOLS, and 302 passed / ruff clean.
fix_batches.scope: redteam/tools/recon.py: 3 read-only, org-scoped gh CLI wrappers (gh_search_code/gh_search_repos/gh_repo_view) via subprocess list argv (no shell), with _OWNER_RE/_REPO_RE (\Z-anchored) + _valid_query + _SCOPE_QUALIFIER_RE + _clamp_limit + _owner_allowed guards, and a total _run_gh (missing/non-zero/timeout/OSError/non-JSON -> structured error). redteam/hooks/scope_guard.py: the 3 names added to _TARGETLESS_TOOLS (GitHub egress is via the authenticated gh CLI, not an engagement scope target). redteam/engagement.py: new optional Scope.github_orgs allowlist (validated against GitHub login grammar) that, when set, binds recon's owner to the engagement.
fix_batches.security_posture: Read-only (subcommands hardcoded; no create/clone/write). Org-scoped per call (owner required; enforced against scope.github_orgs when set). No shell + validated inputs (a value can't be smuggled as a gh flag or shell metachar). Query scope-escape blocked (org:/owner:/user:/repo: refused). The mounted PAT's scope remains the ultimate boundary; the docstring now states this honestly rather than overclaiming.
fix_batches.residual: whois / cert_transparency stay documented stubs (need the whois binary / crt.sh egress; out of scope for #4). Deploy guidance: use a fine-grained, single-org, read-only PAT at /run/secrets/gh_token as the outer boundary. gh not exercised live against the network here; all tests mock subprocess.

fix_batches.tests: tests/test_whitebox_scanners.py (18 cases: pure parser normalisers for semgrep/tfsec/checkov incl. null/list/malformed forms; semgrep_scan + iac_scan argv-building & parse; findings-present non-zero exit -> ok; missing-binary/non-JSON/empty-stdout/timeout -> error; unknown role/kind and tfsec-on-kubernetes rejected without running; run_scanner totality). Full suite 320 passed; ruff clean.
fix_batches.verification: two-agent adversarial pass (scanner correctness/output-schema-fidelity/totality, regression). Reviewer confirmed exit-code semantics (parse regardless of returncode), parser key-fidelity vs the real semgrep/tfsec/checkov JSON schemas, and path/argument safety (scanned path is a resolved asset host_path, scanner allowlisted 3x, list argv). Found + fixed 2 real defects: (F1) json.loads(stdout or '{}') laundered an EMPTY-stdout crash (exit>=2) into a clean 'ok, 0 findings' -> now empty/whitespace stdout is an error (a crashed security scan must never read as clean); (F2) parsers raised TypeError on a truthy non-list results/failed_checks -> _as_list guard. Regression reviewer confirmed all 8 whitebox tools still register, the new optional iac_scan `scanner` enum doesn't break SDK dispatch, targetless entries intact, no circular import, 320 passed / ruff clean.
fix_batches.scope: New redteam/tools/_scanners.py: run_scanner (list argv, no shell; missing/timeout/OSError -> structured error, never raise), load_json (empty/non-JSON -> error, valid JSON at any exit code -> success), parse_semgrep/parse_tfsec/parse_checkov (normalise to {scanner,rule_id,severity,message,path,line}; _as_list/_as_int guards make them total), and scan() dispatch. whitebox.py: semgrep_scan(role) and iac_scan(kind, scanner?) replace the not_implemented stubs, resolve the asset by role/kind, run the scanner, cap findings at 500, attach labels. example.yaml adds semgrep.dev to egress (--config auto rule registry).
fix_batches.totality: Every failure mode returns a structured {status:'error'} dict: missing binary, timeout, OSError, non-JSON output, empty stdout, non-list results/failed_checks, unknown role/kind, unsupported/mismatched scanner. A findings-present run (non-zero exit + valid JSON) is success. No path/argument injection: the scanned path is an operator-supplied asset host_path resolved by build_index; the agent supplies only role/kind/scanner used as lookup filters, and scanner is allowlisted.
fix_batches.residual: sbom_query / openapi_diff / dependency_audit stay documented stubs (not in #6). Scanners not exercised live here (binaries may be absent; tests mock subprocess). Operational: semgrep --config auto needs semgrep.dev egress; the runtime image must install semgrep/tfsec/checkov. Severity vocab differs per scanner (semgrep ERROR/WARNING, tfsec CRITICAL/HIGH/.., checkov often null->UNKNOWN) - each finding carries its `scanner` so consumers know the scheme; cross-scanner normalisation is deferred.

fix_batches.tests: tests/test_jira.py (13 cases: external_key determinism/normalisation/identity + hostile-id sanitisation; severity->priority; jql escaping; issue-fields shape + summary truncation + safe labels; plan_upsert create/update/malformed), tests/test_report_jira.py (10 cases: reporting.jira_project schema, tool gating on atlassian+project, create/update plans, targetless), tests/test_triage_jira.py (4 cases: emit bundle + gating + CLI flag + CLI reject bad project). Full suite 344 passed; ruff clean.
fix_batches.verification: two-agent adversarial pass (security/JQL-injection+idempotency, regression). Regression clean (344 passed, ruff clean, example.yaml still 0 external MCPs). Security reviewer CONFIRMED a real JQL-injection confined to the triage path via two unvalidated inputs: (A) the --jira-project CLI flag bypassed the _JIRA_PROJECT_RE validator that reporting.jira_project has; (B) a tampered ledger's engagement_id flowed unvalidated into external_key -> jql_for_key + Jira labels (ties to the 'tampered ledger must not inject trust' threat model). Both fixed defence-in-depth: engagement_id is sanitised inside external_key (safe space/quote-free label + JQL fragment; identity for schema-valid ids), jql_for_key escapes \ and " in both operands, and the CLI validates --jira-project with _JIRA_PROJECT_RE (exit 2 on bad key). Verified live: the reviewer's two PoC payloads now yield a safe label + balanced JQL literals.
fix_batches.scope: New redteam/jira.py (agent-driven MCP -> harness owns the deterministic scaffolding): external_key(engagement_id,title,location)=redteam-<safe-id>-<sha256[:12]>, severity_to_priority, jql_for_key (escaped), build_issue_fields, plan_upsert (create-vs-update via _match_existing, total against a malformed MCP reply). report.py: gated report__jira_upsert tool (only when an atlassian external_mcp is enabled AND reporting.jira_project is set); added to _TARGETLESS_TOOLS. pipeline/emit.py: emit_report(jira_project=...) also writes <stem>.jira.json (per-kept-finding upsert bundle, same keys as the live tool). engagement.py: optional Reporting.jira_project (validated). cli.py: `redteam triage --jira-project` (validated).
fix_batches.design_note: The Atlassian MCP is agent-driven, so the harness cannot 'call Jira' itself; it produces the deterministic external key + JQL + fields + create/update decision, and the agent (live, via report__jira_upsert) or an operator/automation (offline, via the triage .jira.json bundle) executes the search+create/update through the atlassian__* tools. report.py and triage derive the SAME external key from (engagement_id,title,location) so a re-run updates one ticket per finding. Third-party MCP allowlist stays {atlassian}; GitHub still via gh CLI.
fix_batches.residual: As the reviewer noted, `redteam triage` reads the ledger read-only but does NOT verify its KMS/file seal before triaging (a pre-existing M3 property, out of #5 scope) - the injection is nonetheless neutralised because engagement_id is sanitised and JQL escaped regardless of seal state. Live Jira create/update is agent/operator-driven (no direct REST from the harness); untested against a real Jira here. Applying the .jira.json bundle offline needs a follow-up step (agent or script) that runs the JQL + create/update via the atlassian MCP.

fix_batches.tests: tests/test_otel_provisioning.py (8 cases: Claude Code telemetry env on the redteam service; collector routes metrics->prometheus not tempo; Grafana datasource+dashboard provisioning; dashboard queries claude_code_* metrics; compose wires prometheus + grafana provisioning mounts + tempo config; tempo/prometheus configs valid; add_metric_suffixes<->plain-name lock). Full suite 352 passed; ruff clean. `docker compose config` valid on both the default and --profile dev paths.
fix_batches.verification: two-agent adversarial pass (end-to-end observability wiring, regression). Confirmed authoritative Claude Code OTel env vars + metric names via the claude-code-guide agent (docs.claude.com monitoring). Regression clean: non-dev compose path byte-for-byte intact (services otel-collector+redteam), 352 passed, ruff clean. Wiring reviewer found + we fixed 2 real issues: (HIGH) the collector prometheus exporter's default add_metric_suffixes:true would mangle 3 metric names (claude_code_token_usage_tokens_total, _USD_total, _seconds_total) so those panels showed No-Data -> set add_metric_suffixes:false and query plain claude_code_* names (deterministic dots->underscores); (MED) the Tempo traces panel could never populate (Claude Code traces are beta-gated + app telemetry.py is a no-op tracer, RT-22) -> removed the dead panel but kept the Tempo datasource + traces pipeline wired/ready. Also documented the netpolicy egress gap and refreshed the stale PLAN.md tree.
fix_batches.scope: docker-compose.yml: Claude Code telemetry env on the redteam service; new prometheus service (scrapes the collector) + tempo config mount + grafana provisioning/dashboards mounts; profiles:[dev] preserved. otel/collector.yaml: added a prometheus exporter (add_metric_suffixes:false, resource_to_telemetry_conversion) and routed metrics->[prometheus,debug], traces->[otlp(tempo),debug], logs->[debug]. New otel/tempo.yaml, otel/prometheus.yml, otel/grafana/provisioning/{datasources,dashboards}/*.yaml, otel/grafana/dashboards/redteam-engagement.json (6 populatable Claude Code metric panels). Deleted the superseded import-only otel/grafana_dashboard.json; refreshed docs/PLAN.md tree.
fix_batches.wiring: redteam CLI (OTLP/gRPC) -> otel-collector:4317 -> prometheus exporter :8889 -> prometheus scrape otel-collector:8889 -> Grafana Prometheus datasource http://prometheus:9090 (uid prometheus) -> dashboard panels. Traces -> tempo:4317, Tempo http :3200 (uid tempo) datasource provisioned. All ports/service-names/datasource-uids verified consistent by the reviewer.
fix_batches.residual: Egress: the default-deny nft ruleset (always_allow = api.anthropic.com + engagement egress_allowlist) drops OTLP to the collector in the hardened container; the dev stack needs REDTEAM_NETPOLICY_OPTIONAL=1 or the collector in egress_allowlist (documented; NOT auto-punched, to preserve the security model). Traces are wired but empty until trace export is enabled (Claude Code beta flag or an app TracerProvider - RT-22, still open). Claude Code event-logs go to the collector's debug sink (a Loki datasource is a documented v-next). Not exercised against a live Grafana here; contract-tested + compose config validated.

fix_batches.batch: M3 v-next: environmental CVSS + offensive-priority scoring (build-next #8, part 1)
fix_batches.tests: tests/test_m3_cvss.py (+8 env cases: base-identity for S:U, spec-correct S:C divergence, security-requirement raise, modified-metric lower, monotonicity, garbage) + tests/test_m3_priority.py (18 cases: enrich environmental incl. fallback, prioritize scoring/clamping/chain-membership/zero-env, run_triage integration, CLI --security-requirements parse+accept+reject, emit surfacing). Full suite 373 passed; ruff clean. Live triage over the reference ledger shows a Priority (P1-P4) + Env column and priority in triage.json (SQLi -> P1(93)).
fix_batches.verification: two-agent adversarial pass (CVSS-environmental correctness+totality, regression). The correctness reviewer INDEPENDENTLY cross-checked environmental_score two ways - a from-scratch reimplementation of FIRST.org v3.1 s7.3 over 9984 vectors AND the RedHat `cvss` 3.6 PyPI library over 2400 vectors - with 0 mismatches (constants, MISS cap 0.915, scope-changed 7.52(MISS-0.029)-3.25(MISS*0.9731-0.02)^13, MPR-by-modified-scope, CR/IR/AR multipliers, missing/X->base fallback all spec-correct). prioritize clamps [0,100], is total (None env/base, None confidence, out-of-range chain indices all safe), enrich never raises on garbage/absent vectors. Regression clean: enrich/run_triage new params are keyword-only (no positional misbind); load.py whitelist still excludes cvss_environmental_*/priority_* (tampered ledger can't inject a priority); no test pinned the old 7-col markdown or exact triage.json; 373 passed, ruff clean.
fix_batches.hardening: 3 defects the review found, all fixed: (1) the docstring+test claimed 'env == base with no environmental inputs' as a general identity, but CVSS 3.1's base vs environmental scope-CHANGED impact formulas genuinely differ (base (ISC-0.02)^15 vs env (MISS*0.9731-0.02)^13), so an S:C vector with no inputs legitimately gives env != base (e.g. base 6.9->env 7.0) - corrected the docstring to scope the identity to S:U and rewrote the test to assert the true behavior (S:U identity + spec-correct S:C divergence); (2) a valid base vector with a garbage MODIFIED metric (e.g. MAV:Z) left cvss_environmental_score=None (shown '-') -> enrich now falls back env to the base score; (3) prioritize used `env or base` truthiness which treated a legitimate env score of 0.0 (MC:N/MI:N/MA:N) as missing -> switched to explicit None checks.
fix_batches.scope: redteam/pipeline/cvss.py: environmental_score(vector, requirements) implementing CVSS 3.1 environmental (modified base metrics + CR/IR/AR; temporal treated as Not Defined), + public metrics() helper. models.py: Finding.cvss_environmental_score/rating + priority_score/rating. stages.py: enrich(security_requirements=...) computes environmental; new prioritize(findings, chains) after the chain stage; run_triage threads security_requirements + always prioritizes. cli.py: `redteam triage --security-requirements CR:H,IR:H` (parsed+validated). emit.py: SARIF props (cvssEnvironmental*, priority*) + markdown Priority/Env columns.
fix_batches.residual: Offensive-priority weights are a deliberately-simple, tunable heuristic (env-CVSS*10 base + exploitability<=15 + verify<=15 + chain +20), not a standardised score. Environmental inputs are engagement-wide Security Requirements via the CLI flag, NOT per-asset CMDB data (CMDB integration + temporal metrics remain v-next). The other #8 items (semantic/LLM dedup, multi-backend --verify/--chain routing) are still deferred.

fix_batches.tests: tests/test_m3_semantic_dedup.py (13 cases: same-file reworded merge, cross-file refusal, out-of-range/overlap/self-reference rejection, unparseable/ask-failure degrade, empty-array-not-degraded, run_triage integration, CLI gate refuse/accept, oversized-string-index totality, single-group-object). Full suite 386 passed; ruff clean.
fix_batches.verification: two-agent adversarial pass (false-negative safety + totality, regression). The safety reviewer verified all four safeguards HOLD by tracing + a live battery: (1) nothing vanishes unrecorded (every index in to_drop also appended to dropped, no branch between); (2) disjointness airtight (claimed set; an index can't be canonical in one accepted group and dup in another); (3) same-file guardrail unbypassable (cross-file or no-location dup skipped; canonical-with-no-file skips the group); (4) degraded semantics correct (call-raise/no-payload -> degraded+keep-all; []/zero-valid-groups -> not degraded). Regression clean: run_triage/cli params keyword-only or click-by-name (no misbind); no test pins the old refusal text or timings keys; the default (no --semantic-dedup) path is byte-for-byte unchanged (kept=4 dropped=2); 386 passed, ruff clean.
fix_batches.hardening: 1 CONFIRMED high-severity defect the review found, fixed: _as_index called int(v) on a quoted index with no digit cap, so a model reply with a >4300-digit string index crashed run_triage (Py3.14 int-from-str limit) - violating 'stages must degrade, never crash' (the exact trap models.py already guards with \d{1,9}). Fixed by capping the quoted-index length to <=9 digits + wrapping the _dedup_groups parse defensively. Also fixed a minor consistency gap the reviewer noted: a bare single-group object (not array-wrapped) was rejected AND marked degraded -> now accepted (parity with the chain stage's _chain_items leniency).
fix_batches.scope: redteam/pipeline/stages.py: semantic_dedup_findings(findings, ask, model) - a conservative, total, opt-in model stage that runs between deterministic dedup and verify; _dedup_groups validates model-proposed groups (in-range, disjoint, same-file); merges record DUPLICATE drops with a 'semantic duplicate of ...' detail. run_triage: new keyword-only semantic_dedup param + the stage call + timings + degraded wiring. cli.py: `redteam triage --semantic-dedup` (gated on model_stage_ready alongside --verify/--chain).
fix_batches.design_note: Semantic dedup targets the gap the deterministic (file, vuln_class, line-tolerance) dedup leaves: same-file findings reworded or reclassified (different vuln_class) or just outside the line tolerance. The same-file guardrail is deliberate - two SQLis in different files are DISTINCT findings, not duplicates, and merging them would lose a real vuln; keeping merges same-file + fully recorded in report.dropped makes over-merging auditable and recoverable rather than silently destructive, consistent with M3's drop-and-record philosophy.
fix_batches.residual: Not exercised against a live model here (all tests mock ask; a live run would spend credits and the mocked coverage is comprehensive). Remaining #8: multi-backend model routing (v1 uses the single engagement backend for verify/chain/semantic-dedup) and CMDB per-asset environmental inputs.

findings.title: SSRF to AWS instance-metadata enables role-credential theft
findings.evidence: netpolicy.json always_allow lists 169.254.169.254 (EC2 IMDS). web__http_request uses urllib.request.urlopen which follows 3xx redirects, and only the FIRST url is scope-checked (ctx.assert_in_scope at web.py:42). An in-scope target that 302-redirects to http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ lets a confused/compromised agent read the harness's own AWS workload-role creds (the role used for KMS sealing).
findings.repro: Point an in-scope test server to return 302 Location: http://169.254.169.254/...; web__http_request follows it and returns the metadata body.
findings.fix: Disable auto-redirect in the web pack (custom opener that re-runs the scope check per hop); require IMDSv2 (hop-limit 1, session token); remove 169.254.169.254 from netpolicy always_allow and supply boto3 creds via a path the agent's HTTP tool cannot reach.
findings.fix_note: Fixed and covered by tests in tests/test_fixes_rt01_08.py.

findings.title: redteam run --dry-run is not dry and crashes before the flag is checked
findings.evidence: cli.run constructs Orchestrator before checking dry_run; Orchestrator.__init__ unconditionally mkdirs audit_dir (default /audit, read-only on host) and opens the ledger. The documented smoke test crashes: OSError: Read-only file system: '/audit'.
findings.repro: .venv/bin/redteam run engagements/example.yaml --dry-run  -> OSError /audit
findings.fix: Make --dry-run construct nothing on disk: parse + build_options without instantiating the ledger/audit dir, or gate all filesystem side effects behind 'if not dry_run'. Add a CLI smoke test.
findings.fix_note: Fixed and covered by tests in tests/test_fixes_rt01_08.py.

findings.title: Asset paths resolve against the engagement-file parent, not the repo/clone root
findings.evidence: build_index(host_root=engagement_path.parent) resolves example.yaml's ./targets/example-api to engagements/targets/example-api (doesn't exist) -> FileNotFoundError. In-container the mismatch compounds: compose bind-mounts ./targets at /assets:ro, but the index computes host_path under /engagements and whitebox reads host_path (not container_path), so whitebox can never read the assets even after the path bug is fixed.
findings.repro: .venv/bin/redteam run engagements/example.yaml --dry-run --audit-dir /tmp/x -> FileNotFoundError targets/example-api resolved under engagements/.
findings.fix: Resolve asset paths against a fixed, documented root (repo root / CWD) consistent with where operators clone (./targets) and where compose mounts (/assets); read via container_path in-container; add a test using example.yaml's layout.
findings.fix_note: FIXED (asset root + lenient/strict split). Residual: docker-compose still mounts ./targets at /assets (harmless for host-path whitebox; reconcile when container CLI tools land).

findings.title: SDK hook/agent/option seam is wrong-shaped; the policy gate may not engage
findings.evidence: cli splats build_options() into ClaudeAgentOptions(**options) with no adapter (despite the docstring claiming conversion). Against the real Python Agent SDK: permission_mode='dontAsk' is not a valid mode; PreToolUse must be a HookMatcher list whose callbacks take (input,tool_use_id,context) and return hookSpecificOutput, not a single payload dict returning top-level permissionDecision; SessionStart/SessionEnd are not tool-gating hooks in that shape; agents must be AgentDefinition objects (passed here as raw markdown strings). Net effect is a TypeError at construction or a deny the SDK ignores (fail-open) — catastrophic for a harness whose safety story is 'the hook is the gate'.
findings.repro: Construct ClaudeAgentOptions(**orch.build_options()) against the installed SDK; observe rejection; inspect HookMatcher/AgentDefinition.
findings.fix: Write the real adapter at the _build_hooks seam: wrap hooks in HookMatcher, map to hookSpecificOutput, fix permission_mode, build AgentDefinition objects with per-subagent tool subsets. Add a contract test building real ClaudeAgentOptions from build_options().
findings.fix_note: Fixed and covered by tests in tests/test_fixes_rt01_08.py.

findings.title: In-process MCP tool names get an mcp__ prefix that breaks targetless-allow and the allowlist
findings.evidence: The SDK exposes in-process MCP tools as mcp__<server>__<tool>, and @tool names already embed the pack prefix (e.g. whitebox__repo_read), so the real name is mcp__whitebox__whitebox__repo_read. _TARGETLESS_TOOLS entries and allowed_tools wildcards (whitebox__*) won't match; external 'jira__search' already contains '__' so prefixed_tool_names never adds the 'atlassian' prefix. Fails closed (everything denied) — dead-on-arrival rather than unsafe, but the whole tool surface is unreachable.
findings.repro: Compare @tool registered names vs the tool_name the SDK delivers in PreToolUse payloads.
findings.fix: Centralize tool-name derivation; compute names the SDK actually emits in scope_guard and allowed_tools; add a test pinning the emitted tool_name format.
findings.fix_note: Fixed and covered by tests in tests/test_fixes_rt01_08.py.

findings.title: Scope-guard URL path-prefix match is not canonicalized (out_of_scope deny bypass)
findings.evidence: _UrlPrefixMatcher.matches does raw parsed.path.startswith(path_prefix). With out_of_scope https://staging.example.com/admin: /%61dmin (percent-encoded), //admin (double slash), and /Admin (case) all evade the deny then match the in-scope target. Deny-wins ordering is correct but defeatable by anyone who encodes a URL, including the agent under test.
findings.repro: scope.check('web__http_request', {'url':'https://staging.example.com/%61dmin'}) returns allowed despite out_of_scope /admin.
findings.fix: Canonicalize before matching: lowercase host, percent-decode path, collapse duplicate slashes, resolve dot-segments; add adversarial tests.
findings.fix_note: FIXED + hardened (trailing dots, semicolon params, null-byte, backslash, multi-encoding) and independently re-verified; no realistic-server bypass remains.

findings.title: Empty egress_allowlist silently disables egress checking
findings.evidence: The guard clause 'if host and self._egress_hosts and host not in egress' short-circuits when _egress_hosts is empty, so an omitted/empty egress_allowlist permits any host that matches a target rule — the opposite of PLAN's default-deny. Schema allows the field empty with no warning. Egress is also only checked for URL candidates; bare-host/CIDR targets skip it because _candidate_host returns None.
findings.repro: Engagement with egress_allowlist: [] and a target — egress is never enforced.
findings.fix: Treat empty egress as deny-all (api.anthropic.com implicitly required), or reject empty at parse time; apply the egress check to host/CIDR candidates too.
findings.fix_note: FIXED (empty egress now deny-all except targets). Residual (medium): egress check still only applies to URL candidates, not bare-host/CIDR targets — the 'or' alternative from the original finding.

findings.title: Operator signature is never verified; field optional; chicken-and-egg; principal unbound
findings.evidence: from_yaml never calls SignatureVerifier (documented stub, CLAUDE.md build-next #1), so any unsigned YAML runs. Beyond the wiring: operator_signature is Optional[str]=None with no required check; the signature is embedded in the very YAML it signs (signing changes the bytes — verification can never pass), while example.yaml's own header signs to a DETACHED .sig, contradicting the schema; and nothing binds the ssh -I principal to the operator email, so an operator could present any authorized principal's signature.
findings.repro: Engagement.from_yaml(any unsigned yaml) returns a usable object with no error.
findings.fix: Decide detached-sidecar vs canonical-YAML-minus-signature BEFORE wiring; make the signature required (non-dry-run); bind principal==operator; wire verify into from_yaml and record the result as ledger entry 0.
findings.fix_note: FIXED via detached .sig sidecar verified in the CLI before the orchestrator (fails closed, exit 3); principal bound to operator; embedded field removed.

findings.title: whitebox repo_grep (and asset file-count) follow symlinks out of the read-only mount
findings.evidence: repo_read is safe (_resolve_under_assets resolves symlinks THEN checks containment). repo_grep is not: it rglob()s the repo and read_text()s every file with no symlink/containment check, so a symlink inside an operator-cloned target repo (attacker-influenceable content) pointing at /etc/passwd or /run/secrets/gh_token is read and returned to the agent. assets._count_files rglob likewise follows symlinks. Both whitebox tools are in _TARGETLESS_TOOLS, so the scope-guard gate never inspects them — the tool 'lock' is the only defence and it is missing here.
findings.repro: Plant repo/link -> /run/secrets/gh_token; repo_grep '.*' returns the secret.
findings.fix: Resolve+contain every path repo_grep visits (reuse _resolve_under_assets); skip symlinks pointing outside roots; pass follow/recurse_symlinks=False; add a symlink-escape test.
findings.fix_note: FIXED: repo_grep now skips any file resolving outside the repo root (symlink containment); _count_files skips symlinks. Tested.

findings.title: web__http_request allows write verbs and follows redirects with no enforcement
findings.evidence: _ALLOWED_METHODS includes POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE; the 'no write/delete' rule lives only in prompts, enforced nowhere. urllib follows redirects (see RT-01). A confused/compromised agent can issue destructive HTTP against an in-scope target and be redirected off-scope.
findings.repro: web__http_request(url=in-scope, method='DELETE') is permitted by the tool.
findings.fix: Gate write methods behind an explicit engagement flag (default read-only: GET/HEAD/OPTIONS); disable auto-redirect or re-scope each hop.
findings.fix_note: FIXED: write verbs (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) refused unless scope.allow_write_methods (default False). Read-only by default. Tested.

findings.title: cloud pack is unreachable under the scope guard and advertises GCP/Azure
findings.evidence: cloud__list_buckets/describe_iam take a 'provider' key, not in _TARGET_KEYS and not in _TARGETLESS_TOOLS, so the guard denies them ('called without a target field'). The cloud pack can never run. The provider enum also advertises ['aws','gcp','azure'], contradicting the AWS-only v1 constraint #5.
findings.repro: scope.check('cloud__list_buckets', {'provider':'aws'}) -> denied.
findings.fix: Add cloud tools to _TARGETLESS_TOOLS (and scope-check ARNs inside the tool) or give them a target-bearing key; restrict the enum to aws.

findings.title: Engagement time-window is parsed but never enforced
findings.evidence: Window.covers() is dead code (no caller). PLAN calls the window a 'hard time bound', but an engagement YAML authorizes a run at any time, forever. This is NOT flagged as a stub — engagement.py is on the 'real and tested' list.
findings.repro: grep -rn 'covers(' redteam/ -> only the definition.
findings.fix: Check the window at session start and in _pre_tool_use (deny + ledger entry when outside); decide the naive-timestamp policy explicitly.
findings.fix_note: FIXED: time-window enforced in cli (refuse, exit 4) and on every tool call (_pre_tool_use deny); naive timestamps rejected at parse so covers() can't crash. Tested.

findings.title: redteam-verify exits 0 with only a WARN when no HMAC key is supplied
findings.evidence: PLAN's documented auditor flow is 'redteam-verify <ledger> <seal>' (no key). Without --hmac-key-file, verify prints WARN and returns 0 though it only checked that the seal's head_hash/entry_count match the attacker-recomputable chain. An attacker who truncates the ledger, recomputes head, and rewrites the seal JSON passes with exit 0. The HMAC is the only trust anchor and it is skipped.
findings.repro: redteam-verify ledger.jsonl ledger.seal (no key) -> 'OK ... WARN: signature not checked', exit 0.
findings.fix: Exit non-zero when a seal is present but unverifiable; for KMS seals require kms:VerifyMac and dispatch on seal['method'].
findings.fix_note: FIXED: redteam-verify fails closed when a seal is present but unverifiable (file w/o key->7, kms w/o creds->8, wrong key->6); chain-only->0; dispatches on seal['method']. Tested.

findings.title: Audit named volume is root-owned and shadows the Dockerfile chown; non-root uid cannot write the ledger
findings.evidence: Dockerfile chowns /audit to uid 10001, but the named volume 'audit:' mounts over /audit at runtime created root:root, so uid 10001 cannot write — the first ledger append fails. Classic Docker volume-ownership gotcha.
findings.repro: docker compose up redteam -> PermissionError writing /audit/<id>.jsonl.
findings.fix: Pre-create/chown the volume dir via an init step, set the volume uid, or chown in entrypoint with the needed privilege; add a container smoke test that writes one ledger entry.
findings.fix_note: FIXED: container starts as root, entrypoint chowns the audit volume then drops to redteam via gosu (added to image; CHOWN/SETUID/SETGID caps). Container-only change, not unit-tested. Residual: RT-23 (SDK ~/.claude needs a writable HOME under read_only rootfs) still blocks a full live run.

findings.title: Subagent tool-scoping is not enforced; frontmatter is loaded as a raw prompt string
findings.evidence: _build_subagents reads each .md as a string and passes it as the agent value. The YAML frontmatter 'tools:' (the deliberately narrow set for the dangerous exploiter) is never parsed into an AgentDefinition tool subset — so per-subagent tool restriction is cosmetic and the frontmatter leaks into the prompt as literal text. Frontmatter tool names also wouldn't match SDK names (see RT-05).
findings.repro: Inspect options['agents'] from _build_subagents — a markdown string, not a tools-restricted AgentDefinition.
findings.fix: Parse frontmatter; build AgentDefinition(description, prompt, tools=<mapped subset>); restrict each subagent to its tools.
findings.fix_note: FIXED: subagents are AgentDefinition with a mapped tool subset; empty list stays zero (not inherit-all), malformed tools fail closed, absent=inherit. Tested. (Runtime SDK enforcement of AgentDefinition.tools is assumed per SDK contract.)

findings.title: Dockerfile drops scanner version pins via unquoted shell redirection; unpinned 'latest' binaries; no checksums
findings.evidence: 'pip install --no-cache-dir semgrep>=1.70 checkov>=3.2' is unquoted; the shell parses >=1.70 / >=3.2 as output redirections, so pip installs UNPINNED semgrep/checkov and writes junk files (=1.70/=3.2). tfsec and kube-linter pull releases/latest/download with no checksum/signature; the awscli zip is installed without GPG verification; base python:3.12-slim is not digest-pinned.
findings.repro: Build the image; observe stray files and that the semgrep/checkov floors were ignored.
findings.fix: Quote version specs ('semgrep>=1.70'); pin exact versions + verify checksums/signatures for tfsec/kube-linter/awscli; digest-pin the base image.

findings.title: No locking on ledger/budget; blocking I/O inside async hooks
findings.evidence: LedgerWriter.append and BudgetLedger mutate shared state with no lock. Today append() contains no await, so under single-loop cooperative scheduling it is effectively atomic; but the moment the SDK runs hooks/tools concurrently (threads, or if an await is added inside the critical section) the read-modify-write of _seq/_head_hash and the budget counters race and can corrupt the hash chain or miscount. Separately, synchronous I/O in async paths (os.fsync, urllib.request.urlopen, subprocess.run) stalls the event loop.
findings.repro: Run two _post_tool_use coroutines under a thread pool against one LedgerWriter; observe interleaved seq/head.
findings.fix: Add an asyncio.Lock around append() and budget mutation; move blocking I/O to threads (anyio.to_thread / run_in_executor); document the single-owner invariant if relied upon.

findings.title: Redactor misses standard secrets and over-redacts the audit trail
findings.evidence: The authorization_header pattern requires ':'/'=' immediately after authorization|bearer, so 'Authorization: Bearer eyJ...' is NOT matched (only the separate JWT pattern incidentally catches eyJ tokens; opaque bearer tokens leak to telemetry). The AWS-secret pattern [A-Za-z0-9/+=]{40} matches ANY 40-char token incl every SHA-1/40-hex hash, mass-redacting forensic data — the exact failure the file's own comment forbids. PLAN says the redactor scrubs PII; there is no PII handling at all.
findings.repro: scrub('Authorization: Bearer abc...') leaves it intact; scrub(<any sha1 hex>) redacts it.
findings.fix: Fix the auth pattern to match 'Authorization: <scheme> <token>'; constrain/anchor the AWS-secret pattern (context-aware); add PII detectors (email/SSN/cc/phone) for the telemetry path.

findings.title: RunResult.entry_count reports budget.turns, not the ledger sequence (cosmetic mislabel)
findings.evidence: RunResult.entry_count = self.budget.turns (a tool-call count), not ledger._seq. NOTE: several agents called this 'causes seal verification failure' — that is WRONG. The seal file is written by LedgerWriter.seal() using self._seq, and verify.py compares the seal count to the replayed count; both are consistent. Only the CLI's RunResult summary number is mislabeled.
findings.repro: Compare RunResult.entry_count vs ledger._seq after a run.
findings.fix: Set entry_count from the ledger (_seq / a public accessor).

findings.title: Budget semantics: turns count tool calls, >= boundary, cost may never capture model tokens
findings.evidence: record_turn() fires once per PostToolUse, so max_turns actually caps tool calls (and max_turns is ALSO passed to the SDK as the real turn cap — two meanings). exceeded() uses >= (one extra at the boundary). PostToolUse may run for denied calls, inflating the count. record_cost relies on payload['cost_usd'] from tool events; if the SDK bills model tokens per model turn (not per tool call), max_usd may never accumulate the dominant cost and never trip.
findings.repro: Trace whether cost_usd is delivered on PostToolUse in the SDK; count turns vs tool calls in a run.
findings.fix: Separate 'turns' from 'tool calls'; confirm the SDK cost signal and wire model-token cost; only count successful, allowed calls.

findings.title: SARIF writer is a non-atomic read-modify-write (race + corruption + unbounded growth)
findings.evidence: _append_sarif_result reads, parses, appends, and rewrites the whole SARIF doc per finding. Concurrent findings (subagents) can clobber each other; a crash mid-write corrupts the file; the doc is fully re-serialized each call.
findings.repro: Two concurrent write_finding calls; observe a lost result or truncated JSON on interrupt.
findings.fix: Use an append-only results journal merged at end, or write under a lock to a temp file + atomic rename; bound size.
findings.fix_note: FIXED (M1): _atomic_write_json serializes first (un-encodable obj raises before any file is touched), writes a temp file in the same dir, fsyncs, then os.replace — a crash mid-write can no longer corrupt the SARIF. write_finding runs record_finding + ensure + append under a per-pack asyncio.Lock (correct insurance for a future await in the critical section; RT-17's ledger/budget lock remains separately open). A corrupt base file is quarantined to .corrupt and re-inited so the finding still lands (ledger stays authoritative). Tested in tests/test_m1_report.py.

findings.title: Observability is largely unwired vs the PLAN's claims
findings.evidence: Of the three promised structured events only tool.denied is wired: tool.invoked is never emitted, finding.recorded (event_finding) is never called by the report pack, tool_span is never used, and there is no metrics instrumentation despite the collector's metrics pipeline. No OTel SDK/exporter init exists in code (relies on SDK built-in + env). collector tls insecure:true is unconditional (applies to the prod CENTRAL_OTLP_ENDPOINT), and dev Grafana enables anonymous Admin.
findings.repro: grep for event_finding/tool_span callers (none); inspect the collector tls block.
findings.fix: Emit tool.invoked + finding.recorded; wrap tool execution in tool_span; gate tls insecure to the dev profile; confirm SDK OTel env wiring; align the dashboard to emitted signals.

findings.title: Container hardening gaps: read-only rootfs may break SDK state; CAP_NET_ADMIN as non-root; netpolicy.json dead; secrets always required
findings.status: FIXED (verified) — confirmed end-to-end via docker smoke
findings.evidence: read_only:true with only /tmp and /audit writable likely breaks the SDK's ~/.claude session-JSONL writes that PLAN says are mirrored into the ledger. CAP_NET_ADMIN is granted to render iptables, but the entrypoint runs as uid 10001 and only logs (renders nothing) — default-deny egress is unenforced (CLAUDE.md #3 stub) AND netpolicy.json is consumed by no code (its JSONPath is never evaluated). atlassian_token is mounted unconditionally even when external_mcp is empty, and .secrets/* must exist or compose fails.
findings.repro: Inspect entrypoint (logs only), netpolicy consumers (none), and HOME writability under read_only.
findings.fix: Render egress rules (nft) from the allowlist; give the SDK a writable state dir (tmpfs HOME or point it at /tmp); consume or delete netpolicy.json; mount atlassian_token only when enabled.
findings.fix_note: FIXED: (1) writable SDK state — /home/redteam tmpfs; entrypoint exports HOME + CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR, chowns it, and write-probes it as uid 10001 under read_only (fail-closed exit 71; same for /audit, exit 72). (2) egress — new redteam/runtime/render_netpolicy.py CONSUMES netpolicy.json (evaluates from_engagement JSONPath), renders an nft default-deny ruleset (IMDS dropped before any accept; parsed-address scrub; overlapping allow entries collapsed via collapse_addresses so nft never rejects); entrypoint loads it via `nft -f -` and fails closed (exit 70) unless REDTEAM_NETPOLICY_OPTIONAL=1. (3) netpolicy is rendered from the SAME engagement the CLI runs (single-source the path; CMD ['run'] + appended $ENGAGEMENT_FILE). (4) atlassian_token moved to opt-in docker-compose.atlassian.yml overlay; base no longer requires it. nftables added to the image. Unit-tested in tests/test_fixes_rt23.py (21 cases); real `docker compose run` smoke in tests/container/smoke_rt23.sh confirms read-only boot, HOME/audit writable, in-kernel nft default-deny with IMDS denied, and validate OK as uid 10001. Adversarially verified (3 reviewers): the boot-DoS-on-overlap (F1) and the string-vs-parsed IMDS scrub (F2) were found and fixed. Residual (low): DNS accept is to any host (covert-channel; documented limitation); resolved-hostname IP sets can drift (host netpolicy/SG is the durable backstop).

findings.title: Schema validation gaps: list-size DoS, userinfo URLs, weak hostnames, destination traversal, YAML size
findings.evidence: No max_length on scope.targets/out_of_scope/egress_allowlist (resource DoS via huge lists). URLs with userinfo pass (https://staging.example.com@evil.com -> urlparse hostname is evil.com, so the operator authorizes a different host than intended). _looks_like_hostname/_HOSTNAME_RE accept wildcards, 'a..b', and trailing dots. Reporting.destination is an unchecked Path (traversal/abspath). from_yaml has no input size cap (large/expanding YAML).
findings.repro: Engagement with targets:['https://staging.example.com@evil.com'] authorizes evil.com.
findings.fix: Reject userinfo and unintended ports; tighten the hostname regex; cap list lengths and YAML size; constrain Reporting.destination to the audit dir.

findings.title: Asset containment not enforced: absolute paths and ../ escape host_root
findings.evidence: _resolve_required resolves p relative to host_root but does not assert the result stays under host_root, so an absolute asset path or ../ escapes the intended clone area (operator-controlled, lower risk, but defence-in-depth against a careless/malicious engagement author and pairs badly with RT-09 symlink reads).
findings.repro: assets.source_repos[0].path = /etc -> indexed and grep-able.
findings.fix: After resolve(), assert relative_to(allowed_root); reject absolute/escaping asset paths at parse time.

findings.title: Thin error handling around setup, hooks, and seal
findings.evidence: Orchestrator.__init__ mkdir, report build_pack mkdir/write_text, and CLI run() have no try/except, so setup errors surface as raw tracebacks. Hooks may raise (scope.check, float(cost_usd), record_cost ValueError on negative) instead of returning a deny — if the SDK treats a raising hook as non-blocking, that is fail-open. seal() silently swallows RuntimeError (no HMAC key), hiding a misconfigured production seal.
findings.repro: Feed cost_usd='abc' to _post_tool_use -> ValueError escapes the hook.
findings.fix: Wrap hook bodies to always return a safe decision (default deny on internal error); log+exit cleanly on setup failure; log when seal is skipped for a missing key.
findings.fix_note: PARTIALLY addressed: (1) the PreToolUse hook fails closed (deny) on internal error (earlier batch); (2) M1 wraps the CLI `run` path — Engagement.from_yaml -> 'INVALID' exit 1, Orchestrator/build_options -> 'SETUP ERROR' exit 5 — so setup errors no longer surface as raw tracebacks (tests/test_m1_cli_failclosed.py). STILL OPEN: seal() silently swallows RuntimeError on a missing HMAC key (should log when the prod seal is skipped).

findings.title: External MCP preflight reachability is promised but absent
findings.evidence: The module docstring promises 'runtime preflight checks (egress reachability, ledger record on failure)' and PLAN test #7 expects an mcp.external.unreachable ledger entry, but build_configs only records 'registered' — no reachability check exists. prefixed_tool_names() is dead code.
findings.repro: Enable atlassian with mcp.atlassian.com absent from egress; nothing records unreachable.
findings.fix: Add a preflight connectivity check that records mcp.external.unreachable, or trim the docstring/PLAN claim; remove dead code.

findings.title: Test suite is shallow relative to the 'real and tested' claim
findings.evidence: 27 tests pass but there are zero tests for the orchestrator hooks, CLI, budget.py, redactor, verify.py, CIDR matching, multi-rule deny-wins, egress-deny, window enforcement, symlink escape, or relative-asset resolution. test_kms_seal mocks boto3 (doesn't exercise KMS). The suite passes today with a deny-bypassable scope guard, a dead window check, a broken dry-run, and an unreachable cloud pack — i.e., it does not pin the security-critical behaviours.
findings.repro: Add a test asserting /%61dmin is denied — it fails today.
findings.fix: Add: CLI smoke (RT-02/03), SDK-shape contract (RT-04/05), adversarial scope-guard (RT-06/07), symlink escape (RT-09), window (RT-12), verify exit codes (RT-13), redactor (RT-18), budget (RT-20).

findings.title: Documentation drift would mislead a future session
findings.evidence: README lists 'Auditability — KMS-sealed ... ledger' though KMS isn't wired (file HMAC is the only path). PLAN claims SDK session JSONL is mirrored into the ledger (no such code), Jira upsert + deterministic key (not implemented), and an 11-scenario verification plan that is mostly aspirational. CLAUDE.md build-next ordering predates the confirmed bugs and should be revised (smoke path + SDK seam before auth wiring).
findings.repro: Cross-read each capability claim against code.
findings.fix: Mark KMS/Jira/mirroring/window as not-yet-wired in the feature lists; revise the build-next order (see report).

findings.title: Unused dependencies and missing package metadata
findings.evidence: cryptography>=42 and anyio>=4.3 are declared but unused (ledger uses stdlib hmac/hashlib; auth shells to ssh-keygen). No license/author/classifier metadata. ruff is in dev extras but unconfigured. claude-agent-sdk>=0.1.0 has no upper bound (acceptable pre-1.0 but pin a tested range).
findings.repro: grep imports for cryptography/anyio -> none.
findings.fix: Drop unused deps or start using them; add license/author; pin the SDK to a tested range.

findings.title: Minor code-smells and dead code
findings.evidence: _build_first_party_packs builds a throwaway set instead of reusing tools.PACKS. _extract_target logic is duplicated in orchestrator._extract_target_for_budget and scope_guard._extract_target (drift risk). prefixed_tool_names is dead. whitebox__list_assets returns operator host filesystem paths to the model.
findings.repro: n/a
findings.fix: Reuse PACKS; share one _extract_target; remove dead code; return container paths (not host paths) from list_assets.

refuted.note: It calls ctx.assert_in_scope at web.py:90; recon/web/network all do their own 'lock' check.

refuted.claim: RunResult.entry_count mislabel causes seal verification failure
refuted.note: Seal uses ledger._seq; verify compares the seal count to the replayed count. Only the CLI summary number is wrong (see RT-19).
NamedTemporaryFile creates mode 0600; the signed body is passed via stdin (no temp file); the sig path is unlinked in finally.

refuted.claim: auth temp file not flushed before subprocess
refuted.note: The signature file's context manager exits (closing/flushing) before ssh-keygen reads it.

refuted.claim: file:// / gopher:// SSRF via the web tool
refuted.note: Non-matching scheme/host fails the scope matchers; file:// URLs are denied. The real SSRF vector is redirects from an in-scope https target (RT-01/RT-10).

refuted.claim: the ledger hash chain is forgeable / reorderable
refuted.note: compute_payload_hash binds prev_hash+seq+canonical(payload); reorder/insert/delete/mutate are all detected on replay. Chain construction is sound.

intended_stubs: auth.SignatureVerifier not wired into from_yaml (CLAUDE.md build-next #1) — the absence of wiring is documented; the design flaws around it (RT-08) are not.
KMS sealer not wired; orchestrator uses file HMAC; verify.py has no KMS dispatch (CLAUDE.md #2) — but the verify exit-0-on-no-key trap (RT-13) is a real defect.
entrypoint.sh renders no iptables (CLAUDE.md #3) — but netpolicy.json allow-listing IMDS (RT-01) is a real defect in the template it will render.
recon gh_* / whitebox semgrep|tfsec|checkov / report Jira upsert / cloud bodies return not_implemented (CLAUDE.md #4-6) — expected.
docker-compose references .secrets/* that do not exist yet (README tells the operator to create them).
strengths: Hash-chained ledger (ledger/chain.py) is the strongest file: payload hash binds prev_hash+seq+canonical JSON, fsync per append, resume replays and re-verifies — reorder/insert/delete/mutate all caught.
External-MCP allowlist is enforced twice (parse-time validator + runtime re-assert that raises PermissionError) — exactly the deliberate-code-change posture CLAUDE.md mandates.
Clean module seams: _sdk_shim keeps the package importable without the SDK; ToolContext gives packs a uniform context; policy is centralized in hooks (the documented SDK seam).
recon/web/network tools DO run their own defence-in-depth scope check (assert_in_scope), matching the 'gate + lock' model.
Subagent prompts are well-written on content: scope discipline, read-only rules, confirm-twice, clean hand-off formats (the gap is structural enforcement, RT-15).
Honest documentation discipline (real-vs-stub marking, 'What NOT to do', explicit constraints) is better than most production repos.
build_order: 0. Fix the smoke path so the repo runs end-to-end as documented: make --dry-run construct nothing (RT-02); resolve asset paths against a fixed root and reconcile compose mounts (RT-03); add a CLI smoke test.

1. Prove the SDK seam before anything depends on it: install the SDK in dev extras; add a contract test building real ClaudeAgentOptions from build_options(); write the hook adapter (HookMatcher/hookSpecificOutput), fix permission_mode, AgentDefinition agents, and tool-name prefixes in _TARGETLESS_TOOLS/allowed_tools (RT-04/RT-05). Everything else assumes the gate works.

2. Close the live security gaps: remove IMDS from the egress template + disable/re-scope web redirects + require IMDSv2 (RT-01/RT-10); canonicalize scope-guard URLs (RT-06); make empty egress deny-all (RT-07); contain repo_grep symlinks (RT-09); enforce the time window (RT-12).
3. Wire auth — AFTER deciding detached-vs-embedded signing and binding principal==operator; make the signature required (RT-08).

4. Make the audit trail trustworthy: redteam-verify fails closed without a key + dispatch on seal method (RT-13); add the Sealer protocol and default to KMS in-container (CLAUDE.md #2); add an asyncio.Lock around ledger/budget (RT-17).
5. Container correctness: fix the audit volume ownership (RT-14); writable HOME for SDK state + render egress nft rules (RT-23); pin+verify Dockerfile artifacts (RT-16).

6. Then the existing CLAUDE.md items 4-7 (gh_* tools, Atlassian/Jira upsert, real scanners, OTel dashboard), folding in RT-11 (cloud reachability), RT-18 (redactor+PII), RT-21 (SARIF atomicity), RT-22 (telemetry events).
7. Backfill the test suite (RT-28) alongside each fix so the security-critical behaviours are pinned.
